title: Sandhi Practice Quiz
description: A cross-dictionary skill quiz on Sanskrit sandhi (euphonic combination): the six vowel-sandhi principles, guṇa/vṛddhi grades, visarga and consonant sandhi, internal sandhi, and — the dictionary tie-in — reversing sandhi (sandhi-vigraha) to look a word up.

source.author: Charles Wikner
source.note: Every answer is grounded in Wikner's sandhi lessons (10–11). The dictionary-lookup angle follows the same course's Lesson 14 (split a word with the sandhi rules to find it).

questions.question: What is sandhi, literally and in principle?
questions.answer: Sandhi = 'placing together' — the principle of sounds combining naturally and harmoniously (without awkwardness), for ease of pronunciation. Sanskrit's script is phonetic, so the written notation changes when the sound changes.

questions.question: When does vowel sandhi apply (and when not)?
questions.answer: When a word ending in a vowel is followed by a word beginning with a vowel (in the same sentence or line). A final vowel is NOT changed before a consonant, or in pausa (e.g. at the end of a sentence).

questions.question: Apply vowel sandhi:  a + i  →  ?
questions.answer: e
questions.note: principle 2 — a/ā before a simple vowel other than a takes the guṇa form.

questions.question: Apply vowel sandhi:  a + u  →  ?
questions.answer: o

questions.question: Apply vowel sandhi:  a + a  →  ?
questions.answer: ā
questions.note: principle 1 — the same simple vowel (short or long) doubles to its long (dīrgha) measure.

questions.question: Apply vowel sandhi:  i + i  →  ?
questions.answer: ī

questions.question: Apply vowel sandhi:  ā + i  →  ?
questions.answer: e
questions.note: principle 2 applies whether the leading a is short or long.

questions.question: Apply vowel sandhi:  a + e  →  ?
questions.answer: ai
questions.note: principle 3 — a/ā before a guṇa or vṛddhi sound takes the vṛddhi form.

questions.question: Apply vowel sandhi:  a + o  →  ?
questions.answer: au

questions.question: Apply vowel sandhi:  i + a  →  ?
questions.answer: ya
questions.note: principle 4 — a simple vowel other than a, before a different vowel, becomes the semivowel (antaḥstha) of the same mouth position (i→y, u→v, ṛ→r, ḷ→l).

questions.question: Apply vowel sandhi:  u + a  →  ?
questions.answer: va

questions.question: Apply vowel sandhi:  e + a  →  ?
options: e (a elided, written with an avagraha: e ’)
questions.answer: e (a elided, written with an avagraha: e ’)
questions.explanation: principle 6 — when e or o is followed by a short a, the a is elided and replaced by an avagraha (e ’, o ’).

questions.question: Principle 4: a simple vowel other than a, before a different vowel, is replaced by what?
questions.answer: The antaḥstha (semivowel) of the same mouth position: i/ī → y, u/ū → v, ṛ → r, ḷ → l. (e.g. i + V → yV.)

questions.question: What is the guṇa grade of i / ī (and of u / ū, and ṛ)?
questions.answer: guṇa of i/ī = e; of u/ū = o; of ṛ = ar. (guṇa 'strengthens' a simple vowel by adding a measure of a.)

questions.question: What is the vṛddhi grade of i / ī (and of u / ū, and a)?
questions.answer: vṛddhi of i/ī = ai; of u/ū = au; of a = ā. (vṛddhi adds a further measure of a on top of guṇa.)

questions.question: What is the guṇa of u?
questions.answer: o

questions.question: How does Pāṇini define the guṇa and vṛddhi vowels?
questions.answer: guṇa = a, e, o;  vṛddhi = ā, ai, au.

questions.question: Visarga sandhi — apply:  rāmaḥ + gacchati  →  ?
questions.answer: rāmo gacchati
questions.note: final -aḥ before a voiced (ghoṣa) consonant becomes -o.

questions.question: Visarga sandhi — apply:  rāmaḥ + āgataḥ  →  ?
questions.answer: rāma āgataḥ
questions.explanation: before a vowel other than a short a, the -s of -aḥ is simply dropped, leaving -a (the two words stay separate: rāma āgataḥ).

questions.question: What happens to final -aḥ before a short a?
questions.answer: It becomes -o, and the following short a is elided and replaced by an avagraha — so -aḥ + a → -o ’ (e.g. rāmaḥ + atra → rāmo ’tra).

questions.question: What does a final s or r become before an unvoiced (aghoṣa) sound or a pause?
questions.answer: A visarga (ḥ). Exception: before c/ch, ṭ/ṭh, or t/th it is replaced by the sibilant (ś, ṣ, or s) of that consonant's mouth position.

questions.question: What does a final -m become before any consonant?
questions.answer: An anusvāra (ṃ).

questions.question: What happens to a final unvoiced -k, -ṭ, -t, -p before a voiced (ghoṣa) sound?
questions.answer: Each is replaced by the voiced (ghoṣa) consonant of the same mouth position: -k→-g, -ṭ→-ḍ, -t→-d, -p→-b. (Before an aghoṣa sound they stay unchanged.)

questions.question: A word ends in a short vowel and the next word begins with ch-. What is inserted?
questions.answer: A c — so -V + ch- → -V cch- (the ch is doubled to cch).

questions.question: Before a pause (avasana), a word may end in only one of eight consonants. Which?
questions.answer: k, ṭ, t, p, ṅ, n, m, or ḥ (visarga) — or any vowel except ṛ and ḷ.

questions.question: Internal sandhi: after which sounds is a following s replaced by ṣ?
questions.answer: After k, r, i, ī, u, ū, ṛ, ṝ, e, ai, o, au — even with an intervening anusvāra or visarga — unless the s is final or followed by r. (This is why endings like -su appear as -ṣu after such stems.)

questions.question: Internal sandhi: after which sounds is a following n replaced by ṇ?
questions.answer: After ṣ, r, ṛ, ṝ (even across intervening ka-/pa-varga, y, v, h, or a vowel) — when the n is followed by a vowel, m, v, y, or n.

questions.question: Why does sandhi matter for using a dictionary?
questions.answer: Words join by sandhi in real text, but dictionaries list the unjoined stems. To find a word inside a compound or sentence you must REVERSE the sandhi (sandhi-vigraha) to recover the separate padas, then look each up — exactly the 'split it with the sandhi rules' technique for hard words.

questions.question: A dictionary/​paradigm cites a noun with a final visarga (e.g. rāmaḥ). What must you remember before applying external sandhi in a sentence?
questions.answer: Citation forms already have the to-a-pause (avasana) sandhi applied, so a final visarga usually stands for a final -s. Replace the visarga with s, then apply the visarga-sandhi rule for the actual following sound.

questions.question: Samprasāraṇa: a semivowel is replaced by the simple vowel of its mouth position (and the following vowel elided). How does this trip up a dictionary lookup — e.g. for the dhātu yaj?
questions.answer: Derived forms can hide the root: yaj appears as ij- (ij-ya), svap as sup- (sup-ta), vac as uc- (uc-atha), prach as pṛcch- (pṛcch-ati). You may have to undo samprasāraṇa to recognise the dhātu the dictionary lists.
